title: Version Control Basics
intro: Git keeps your code safe and lets you undo mistakes. Think of commits like save points in a video game — if the boss fight goes wrong, you can reload instead of starting over. Swipe right on good git habits, left on practices that will cause you pain.

teach.title: Git Is Your Safety Net
teach.body: Git tracks every change you make to your code. If you break something, you can go back to a working version. If you want to try a risky feature, you can create a branch so your main code stays safe. Without git, one bad change can destroy hours of work with no way to recover.

teach.title: Save Points in a Video Game
teach.body: Git commits are like save points in a video game. Before you fight the boss (deploy a feature), you save your progress. If the boss kills you (the feature breaks), you reload from the last save instead of starting the entire game over. Players who never save lose everything when things go wrong. Don't be that player.

teach.title: The Velvet Rope at the Club
teach.body: A `.gitignore` is like the bouncer with a velvet rope at a nightclub. It has a list of names that are NOT getting in — node_modules, .env files, build output. Without the bouncer, every random file wanders into your repository and causes chaos. The bouncer doesn't care how important those files think they are; if they're on the list, they stay outside.

teach.title: The .gitignore Is Non-Negotiable
teach.body: A `.gitignore` file tells git which files NOT to track. You never want to commit `node_modules/` (thousands of dependency files), `.env` files (secret API keys), or `.next/` (build output). create-next-app generates a `.gitignore` for you, but always double-check it before your first commit.

Version control isn't just a developer skill — it's a business skill. When you're shipping updates to paying users, git lets you roll back safely if something breaks. Your users stay happy, your revenue stays intact.

Initialize git immediately when you start a project. Commit early, commit often, and write clear commit messages. A good .gitignore protects you from accidentally sharing secrets or bloating your repository.

cards.content: Make small, frequent commits after completing each meaningful piece of work
cards.explanation: Small commits are easy to understand, easy to review, and easy to revert. If something breaks, you only lose a small amount of work.

cards.content: Commit your node_modules folder so teammates don't have to run npm install
cards.explanation: node_modules contains thousands of files and can be hundreds of megabytes. It should always be in .gitignore. Teammates run npm install to get their own copy.

cards.content: Write commit messages like 'Add task creation form with title and description fields'
cards.explanation: Descriptive messages tell you exactly what changed without opening the code. When you need to find when a feature was added, clear messages are invaluable.

cards.content: Commit your .env file so your API keys and database URLs are backed up in the repo
cards.explanation: Never commit .env files. They contain secrets like API keys and database passwords. If your repo is ever public, those secrets are exposed. Use .env.example with placeholder values instead.

cards.content: Check your .gitignore before making your very first commit to make sure secrets and build files are excluded
cards.explanation: It's much harder to remove a file from git history after it's been committed. Checking .gitignore first prevents accidental commits of sensitive files.

cards.content: Write commit messages like 'fix stuff' or 'update' or 'asdfgh'
cards.explanation: Vague messages are useless when you're trying to understand what changed. Three months from now, 'fix stuff' tells you nothing. Take 10 seconds to write a real message.

cards.content: Create a .env.example file with placeholder values so new developers know which environment variables they need
cards.explanation: A .env.example file documents which variables the project needs without exposing real values. New developers copy it to .env and fill in their own keys.
